Thousands of calls each prepend the same large, rarely-changing document. Cost and latency are the complaint. Which change helps most?

practice: Apply prompt caching (cache_control) to the static document prefix so it is not reprocessed each call.
Mark the unchanging prefix with a cache breakpoint to reuse it across calls.
timed: Set a cache_control breakpoint on the stable prefix so repeated context is served from cache.

practice: Switch the whole pipeline to the smallest model to cut cost.
timed: Move everything to the cheapest model tier.
Downgrade the entire pipeline to the smallest model.

practice: Truncate the document to its first page on every call.
timed: Send only the first page of the document each time.
Clip the document to page one for every request.

practice: Request a rate-limit increase to push more calls through.
timed: Ask for higher rate limits to raise throughput.
Get the rate limit bumped to send more calls.

d5-003.explanation: A large, static, frequently-reused prefix is the textbook prompt-caching case - cache_control cuts latency and cost without losing context. Downgrading the model risks quality, truncation loses information, and a rate-limit bump addresses throughput not the per-call waste. Judgment: match the fix to the actual cost driver.

stem: A RAG bot indexes each 50-page document as one chunk and stuffs the top 5 whole documents into the prompt. Answers are vague and miss specifics. What is the core fix?
A retrieval system uses whole 50-page documents as single chunks; responses are unfocused and lose detail. Which change addresses the root cause?

practice: Chunk documents into smaller, semantically coherent passages and retrieve/rank those, so the relevant specifics surface.
Split documents into focused passages and retrieve at passage granularity.
timed: Index smaller, meaning-coherent chunks and retrieve those passages instead of whole documents.

practice: Retrieve more whole documents per query.
timed: Pull back additional full documents each query.
Increase the number of whole documents retrieved.

practice: Raise the temperature so answers are more specific.
timed: Increase temperature to sharpen specificity.
Turn temperature up for more detailed answers.

practice: Switch to a larger-context model and keep whole-document chunks.
timed: Adopt a bigger context window and retain full-document chunks.
Use a larger-context model without re-chunking.

d5-004.explanation: Whole-document chunks bury the relevant passage and dilute retrieval; the core fix is smaller, semantically coherent chunks ranked at passage level. Retrieving more whole docs worsens dilution, temperature does not add precision, and a bigger context still feeds the model unfocused chunks.

stem: A long-running assistant starts giving inconsistent answers to the same question as the session grows. Which practice best maintains consistency?
Over a long session, an assistant drifts and contradicts its earlier answers. What is the recommended way to keep it consistent?

practice: Maintain a stable, re-supplied summary of the durable facts and decisions, and trim volatile, low-relevance history.
Anchor key decisions/facts in a compact summary that is carried forward, dropping noisy history.
timed: Keep a concise running summary of settled facts and decisions in context, pruning the volatile history around it.

practice: Rely on the full verbatim transcript so nothing is ever lost.
timed: Retain the entire transcript verbatim to be safe.
Keep every prior message in full so context is complete.

practice: Raise the temperature so answers vary less.
timed: Increase temperature to reduce variation.
Turn temperature up for steadier answers.

practice: Restart the session before each question to avoid drift.
timed: Begin a fresh session for every question.
Reset the conversation ahead of each query.

d5-005.explanation: Consistency over long sessions comes from anchoring durable facts/decisions in a stable summary while trimming noise. A full verbatim transcript bloats and dilutes; higher temperature increases variance; restarting each time discards the context that makes answers coherent.

stem: Under load, a service receives 429 and 529 responses and immediately retries in a tight loop, which makes the overload worse. What is the right handling?
When the API returns 429/529 errors, an app retries instantly and repeatedly, amplifying the overload. Which approach is correct?

practice: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, respect any retry-after hint, and cap the number of attempts.
Back off exponentially with jitter, honor retry-after, and bound retries.
timed: Use capped exponential backoff with jitter and respect the retry-after header instead of immediate retries.

practice: Retry immediately and more aggressively to get through sooner.
timed: Retry faster and harder to push past the limit.
Hammer retries immediately to break through.

practice: Ignore the errors and silently drop the affected requests.
timed: Swallow the errors and discard those requests.
Suppress the 429/529s and drop the calls.

practice: Switch to a different model on every 429 response.
timed: Change models each time a 429 occurs.
Hop to another model on every rate-limit error.

d5-006.explanation: Rate-limit/overload errors call for capped exponential backoff with jitter and respect for retry-after. Immediate aggressive retries worsen the overload; dropping requests loses work silently; swapping models per 429 is a non-fix that ignores the throttling signal.

stem: A user-facing chat shows a long blank pause before the full answer appears. Which change best improves perceived responsiveness?
Users wait through a blank delay until a chat reply fully arrives. What most improves the perceived latency?

practice: Stream the response incrementally as it is generated, handling partial chunks in the UI.
Use streaming so tokens render as they arrive instead of after completion.
timed: Stream the output token-by-token so the user sees it build, processing partial chunks.

practice: Switch to a larger model to produce the answer faster.
timed: Move to a bigger model for speed.
Use a more capable model to cut the wait.

practice: Increase max_tokens so the answer is ready sooner.
timed: Raise the max_tokens limit to speed things up.
Bump max_tokens so it finishes faster.

practice: Raise the temperature to make generation quicker.
timed: Increase temperature for faster output.
Turn temperature up to speed generation.

d5-007.explanation: Streaming improves perceived responsiveness by rendering tokens as they generate (the UI must handle partial chunks). A bigger model is not necessarily faster and adds cost; max_tokens sets a ceiling, not speed; temperature does not affect latency.

stem: A pipeline has a complex multi-constraint planning step that produces shallow, error-prone output and a trivial formatting step that is fine. Where should you spend an extended-thinking budget?
Given one hard reasoning step and one simple formatting step, where is extended thinking worth enabling?

practice: Enable extended thinking on the hard multi-constraint reasoning step, not the trivial formatting step.
Spend the thinking budget on the complex planning step and skip it on formatting.
timed: Turn on extended thinking only for the demanding reasoning step, leaving the simple step without it.

practice: Enable it on every step for consistency.
timed: Apply thinking uniformly to all steps.
Switch it on everywhere for consistency.

practice: Avoid extended thinking entirely to keep costs down.
timed: Never use thinking, to minimize cost.
Disable it across the board for savings.

practice: Enable it on the formatting step, since output quality matters most there.
timed: Put the thinking budget on the formatting step.
Use extended thinking for the formatting work.

d5-008.explanation: Extended thinking pays off on genuinely hard reasoning, so spend it on the multi-constraint planning step and not the trivial one. Enabling it everywhere wastes cost/latency, banning it leaves the hard step weak, and putting it on formatting targets the step that does not need it.

stem: To prove a new RAG pipeline is "better," a team reports it "feels more accurate" after trying 10 hand-picked questions. What is the right critique?
A team claims a revised pipeline is an improvement based on a good impression from 10 cherry-picked queries. How should you respond?

practice: Measure it on a representative held-out eval set with defined metrics (e.g. grounded-answer accuracy, citation correctness) over many cases, using code- and/or model-based grading.
Build a representative test set with explicit metrics and grade many cases automatically rather than relying on impressions.
timed: Evaluate against a held-out, representative dataset with concrete metrics scored across many cases, not a hand-picked anecdote.

practice: Accept it — if it feels more accurate, it is more accurate.
timed: Trust the impression that it feels better.
Take the "feels more accurate" judgment at face value.

practice: Just try more hand-picked questions until confidence is high.
timed: Keep adding cherry-picked questions until convinced.
Hand-select more examples to build confidence.

practice: Only latency matters for a pipeline, so measure that instead.
timed: Measure latency alone, since that is what counts.
Evaluate only speed and ignore accuracy.

d5-009.explanation: A reliable comparison needs a representative held-out eval set with defined metrics graded over many cases — not an impression from cherry-picked questions. "Feels more accurate" is not measurement, more hand-picked cases compound selection bias, and latency-only ignores the accuracy claim being made.
